metadata.title: Homeostasis During Fasting. II. Hormone Substrate Differences Between Men and Women
metadata.journal: Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
metadata.study_design: Comparative fasting study with controlled intervention
metadata.demographics: Ten normal men and 9 normal women; Four men matched with 4 women in weight and age; Five women fasted in both preovulatory and postovulatory phases

evidence.quote: Ten normal men and 9 normal women were fasted for 72 hr.
Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time while receiving ethinylestradiol (0.1 mg twice daily).
Five of the women were fasted in both the preovulatory and postovulatory phases of the menstrual cycle.
The mean lowest glucose concentration in plasma in the male group during the 3 days of starvation was 66.4 ± 2.9 mg/100 ml. The corresponding figure in females was 47.8 ±2.9 mg/100 ml (p < 0.01).
Mean peak glucagon concentrations in women and men were, respectively, 230.0 ± 36.1 mig/ml and 121.8 ± 17.9 nng/ml (p < 0.01).
Ten normal men and 9 normal women were fasted for 72 hr.
Samples were obtained at 8 AM and 8 PM daily.
Each 3-day fast was preceded by a 2-day control period.

methods_participants.label: Glucose assay - coefficient of variation between assays

evidence.quote: The coefficient of variation between triplicates in a given assay was 1.5%

methods_participants.label: Insulin assay method
methods_participants.value: modifications of Berson and Yalow method

evidence.quote: Serum immunoreactive insulin, HGH and glucagon were assayed by modifications of the methods of Berson and Yalow
No subject had CHO intolerance and hepatic screening tests were normal.
Prior to admission, all subjects ingested a diet containing a minimum of 150 g of carbohydrate
Within 10% of ideal body weight according to New York Metropolitan Tables.

methods_participants.label: Menstrual cycle timing - postovulatory
methods_participants.value: 15 to 17 days after last day of menstrual bleeding

evidence.quote: the latter, 15 to 17 days after the last day of menstrual bleeding
For glucagon measurements, samples were run in triplicate with appropriate control tubes for each sample.

narrative_results.statement: At 36, 48 and 60 hr of the fast, plasma glucagon concentrations were significantly higher in women than in men (p < 0.01)

evidence.quote: With prolonged fasting, plasma glucose and insulin concentrations decreased in both men and women, reaching a plateau on the second or third day of the fast.

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 describes insulin sample replicates (4), while Pass 2 describes insulin assay maximum variation in basal range (1.0 μU/ml). These are different measurement attributes.
Pass 1 describes glucagon sample replicates (3), while Pass 2 describes HGH assay maximum variation (0.5 ng/ml). These are completely different assays and measurements.
Pass 1 describes HGH low range maximum variation (0.5 ng/ml), while Pass 2 describes HGH 5-15 range maximum variation (1.0 ng/ml). These are different ranges with different values.

Pass 1 describes HGH high range maximum variation (1.0 ng/ml with Berson and Yalow method), while Pass 2 describes HGH method (modifications of Roth, Click, Yalow and Berson). Different method attribution and measurement type.

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Four men, matching 4 of the women in weight and age, were also fasted a second time

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: In women, plasma glucose decreased to 52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr vs 67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml in men.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: In women, plasma glucose decreased to 52.5 ±3.4 mg/100 ml at 60 hr vs 67.5 ±2.0 mg/100 ml in men. The difference between men and women while fasting was significant at the 1% level.

semantic_groups.rationale: Identical participant count for women
Identical fasting duration specification
Same dosing information (0.1 mg twice daily) with different field segmentation; pass_2 has redundant 'mg' unit
Pass_1 describes menstrual cycle phases studied; pass_2 appears to be a count (5) rather than phase names - these are different data types
Both describe twice-daily blood sampling; pass_1 specifies times (8 AM and 8 PM) while pass_2 only states frequency
Identical control period duration of 2 days
Both identify glucose oxidase method; pass_2 adds measurement detail (triplicate) not present in pass_1
Both report within-assay coefficient of variation of 1.5%; pass_1 includes redundant method reference
Both describe glucose assay coefficient of variation between assays at 2.5%. Pass 1 includes method (glucose oxidase) which is additional detail but same core fact.
Both identify Trout, Estes and Friedberg method for FFA assay. Pass 2 adds replication detail (measured in duplicate) not present in Pass 1, but core method is same.
Both identify modifications of Berson and Yalow method for Insulin assay. Pass 2 adds replication detail (quadruplicate) not in Pass 1, but core method is same.
Pass 1 describes insulin sample replicates (4), while Pass 2 describes insulin assay maximum variation in basal range (1.0 μU/ml). These are different measurement attributes.
Both identify charcoal separatory method for glucagon assay. Pass 2 adds replication detail (triplicate) not in Pass 1, but core method is same.
Pass 1 describes glucagon sample replicates (3), while Pass 2 describes HGH assay maximum variation (0.5 ng/ml). These are completely different assays and measurements.
Pass 1 describes HGH low range maximum variation (0.5 ng/ml), while Pass 2 describes HGH 5-15 range maximum variation (1.0 ng/ml). These are different ranges with different values.

Pass 1 describes HGH high range maximum variation (1.0 ng/ml with Berson and Yalow method), while Pass 2 describes HGH method (modifications of Roth, Click, Yalow and Berson). Different method attribution and measurement type.
Both state no subject had CHO/carbohydrate intolerance as inclusion criterion. Pass_2 uses abbreviation 'CHO' and 'No' vs pass_1's full phrase, but convey identical information.
Both state no subject receiving medication as inclusion criterion. Pass_1 uses 'No subject receiving medication' vs pass_2's 'None', but both convey the same exclusion criterion.
Both specify 150g minimum carbohydrate in prefasting diet. Pass_1 states '150 g minimum' while pass_2 states 'minimum 150 g' - same fact, minor word order difference.
Identical specification: 1 g/kg body weight protein in prefasting diet. Perfect match between both extractions.
Both specify 2 weeks prefasting diet duration. Pass_1 includes 'minimum' while pass_2 omits it, but the core fact is identical.

Both specify within 10% of ideal body weight criterion. Pass_1 labels it 'Weight matching criteria' while pass_2 labels it 'Inclusion criterion - body weight', but the actual requirement is identical.

Both specify preovulatory phase timing as 4 to 5 days after last day of menstrual bleeding. Pass_1 uses 'Menstrual cycle timing - preovulatory' while pass_2 uses 'Preovulatory phase timing', but the timing specification is identical.

Both specify postovulatory phase timing as 15 to 17 days after last day of menstrual bleeding. Pass_1 uses 'Menstrual cycle timing - postovulatory' while pass_2 uses 'Postovulatory phase timing', but the timing specification is identical.
Both extractions report identical participant count for men: 10
Pass 1 has no extraction while Pass 2 reports 4 weight-matched participant pairs; information only present in one extraction
